channel.title: Green-Tea on zhouyang — The 25th Frame
channel.description: Recent content in Green-Tea on zhouyang — The 25th Frame

item.title: The Six Types of Chinese Tea: A Complete Guide for People Who Just Want Good Tea
item.description: I’ve spent nearly thirty years in China, and in that time I’ve drunk more tea than I can count. Green tea from a porcelain gaiwan in a Hangzhou teahouse. White tea from a chipped mug in a Fujian farmer’s kitchen. Pu’er pressed into a cake older than I am. Tea that cost more than a nice dinner, and tea that came in a crinkled plastic bag from a street-corner shop for twelve yuan. I’ve met collectors who talk about vintages the way sommeliers talk about Bordeaux, and old men in parks who brew the same jasmine blend every morning in a pickle jar and wouldn’t trade it for anything.

item.title: Longjing: One Leaf, One Spring
item.description: The first time I saw Longjing leaves hit hot water, I was twenty-three and standing in a tea house on the southern shore of West Lake. The tea master — a woman in her seventies with hands that looked carved from cypress — poured from a long-spout kettle in a single continuous arc. The leaves didn’t float. They stood upright. One by one, pale green needles rose from the bottom of the glass like a forest coming awake. It was February. The air outside was forty degrees. Inside, the glass was seventy degrees and smelled like roasted chestnuts and fresh-cut grass and something else I couldn’t name. I asked her what the something else was.
